section.title: The Northern Tribes
section.sectionRationale: The Chronicler moves quickly through Issachar, a partial record of Benjamin, a single verse for Naphtali, Manasseh east and west of the Jordan, Ephraim, including the sudden death of two of his sons and the grief-born birth of Beriah, and finally Asher, closing the survey of the tribes descended from Jacob's sons other than Levi and Judah.
section.thesis: By recording, however briefly, every one of the remaining northern tribes, even pausing over Ephraim's raw grief and the small mercy of Beriah's birth, the Chronicler shows the returned exiles that no tribe, however scattered by war and exile of its own, has fallen outside the record of a God who remembers every family descended from Israel.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the descendants of Issachar; the descendants of Benjamin; the descendants of Naphtali; the descendants of Manasseh; Ephraim's grief and the birth of Beriah; and the descendants of Asher.

units.label: The descendants of Issachar
units.note: mighty men of valor, numbered by the thousands

verses.text: The sons of Issachar: Tola, Puah, Jashub, and Shimron, four in all.

The sons of Tola: Uzzi, Rephaiah, Jeriel, Jahmai, Ibsam, and Shemuel, the heads of their {{mishpachah|families}}. In the days of David, 22,600 descendants of Tola were numbered in their genealogies as {{gibbor|mighty men of valor}}.
The son of Uzzi: Izrahiah. The sons of Izrahiah: Michael, Obadiah, Joel, and Isshiah. All five of them were chiefs.
In addition to them, according to their genealogy, they had 36,000 troops for battle, for they had many wives and children.
Their kinsmen belonging to all the families of Issachar who were mighty men of valor totaled 87,000, as listed in their genealogies.

units.label: The descendants of Benjamin
units.note: a partial record of a large tribe

verses.text: The three sons of Benjamin: Bela, Becher, and Jediael.
The sons of Bela: Ezbon, Uzzi, Uzziel, Jerimoth, and Iri, heads of their families, five in all. There were 22,034 mighty men of valor listed in their genealogies.
The sons of Becher: Zemirah, Joash, Eliezer, Elioenai, Omri, Jeremoth, Abijah, Anathoth, and Alemeth; all these were Becher's sons.
Their genealogies were recorded according to the heads of their families, 20,200 mighty men of valor.
The son of Jediael: Bilhan. The sons of Bilhan: Jeush, Benjamin, Ehud, Chenaanah, Zethan, Tarshish, and Ahishahar.
All these sons of Jediael were heads of their families, mighty men of valor; there were 17,200 fit for battle.
The Shuppites and Huppites were descendants of Ir, and the Hushites were descendants of Aher.

units.label: The descendants of Naphtali
units.note: one verse for a whole tribe

verses.text: The sons of Naphtali: Jahziel, Guni, Jezer, and Shallum, the descendants of Bilhah.

units.label: The descendants of Manasseh
units.note: east of the Jordan, in Gilead

verses.text: The descendants of Manasseh: Asriel through his Aramean concubine. She also gave birth to Machir the father of Gilead.
Machir took a wife from among the Huppites and Shuppites. The name of his sister was Maacah. Another descendant was named Zelophehad, who had only daughters.
Machir's wife Maacah gave birth to a son, and she named him Peresh. His brother was named Sheresh, and his sons were Ulam and Rekem.
The son of Ulam: Bedan. These were the sons of Gilead son of Machir, the son of Manasseh.
His sister Hammolecheth gave birth to Ishhod, Abiezer, and Mahlah.
And these were the sons of Shemida: Ahian, Shechem, Likhi, and Aniam.

units.label: Ephraim's grief and the birth of Beriah
units.note: sons killed stealing livestock, and a son born in tragedy

verses.text: The descendants of Ephraim: Shuthelah, Bered his son, Tahath his son, Eleadah his son, Tahath his son,
Zabad his son, and Shuthelah his son. Ezer and Elead were killed by the natives of Gath, because they went down to steal their livestock.
Their father Ephraim {{avel|mourned}} for many days, and his relatives came to comfort him.
And again he slept with his wife, and she conceived and gave birth to a son. So he {{shem|named}} him Beriah, because tragedy had come upon his house.
His daughter was Sheerah, who built Lower and Upper Beth-horon, as well as Uzzen-sheerah.
Additionally, Rephah was his son, Resheph his son, Telah his son, Tahan his son,
Ladan his son, Ammihud his son, Elishama his son,
Nun his son, and Joshua his son.
Their holdings and settlements included Bethel and its villages, Naaran to the east, Gezer and its villages to the west, and Shechem and its villages as far as Ayyah and its villages.
And along the borders of Manasseh were Beth-shean, Taanach, Megiddo, and Dor, together with their villages. The descendants of Joseph son of Israel lived in these towns.

units.label: The descendants of Asher
units.note: choice and mighty men, chiefs among the leaders

verses.text: The children of Asher: Imnah, Ishvah, Ishvi, Beriah, and their sister Serah.
The sons of Beriah: Heber, as well as Malchiel, who was the father of Birzaith.
Heber was the father of Japhlet, Shomer, and Hotham, and of their sister Shua.
The sons of Japhlet: Pasach, Bimhal, and Ashvath. These were Japhlet's sons.
The sons of Shemer: Ahi, Rohgah, Hubbah, and Aram.
The sons of his brother Helem: Zophah, Imna, Shelesh, and Amal.
The sons of Zophah: Suah, Harnepher, Shual, Beri, Imrah,
Bezer, Hod, Shamma, Shilshah, Ithran, and Beera.
The sons of Jether: Jephunneh, Pispa, and Ara.
The sons of Ulla: Arah, Hanniel, and Rizia.

All these were the descendants of Asher, heads of their families, choice and mighty men of valor, and chiefs among the leaders. The number of men fit for battle, recorded in their genealogies, was 26,000.

mishpachah.gloss: Clan or extended family. Even Issachar, a tribe rarely mentioned elsewhere in Scripture, has its heads of families and thousands of warriors carefully recorded, showing that every mishpachah within Israel, however minor its later reputation, mattered enough to be counted and named.

gibbor.gloss: Mighty man of valor, a warrior of strength and standing. The phrase recurs like a refrain across this chapter, for Issachar, Benjamin, and Asher alike, a repeated reminder that these lesser-known tribes were nonetheless real and formidable, contributing real strength to Israel's defense even though their stories are rarely told elsewhere.

avel.gloss: To mourn, to grieve. Ephraim mourned for many days after his sons Ezer and Elead were killed, and his relatives came to comfort him, an unusually personal and emotional moment inside an otherwise dry genealogical record, showing that even ancient family trees are made of real grief and real loss.

shem.gloss: Name. Ephraim named his newborn son Beriah, a name the BSB footnote explains sounds like the Hebrew word for tragedy or disaster, so that every time the name was spoken it carried the memory of the sons lost and the grief that preceded the child's birth, a naming that turned sorrow itself into a permanent, spoken memorial.

basic.context: This chapter moves briskly through several tribes that receive little space elsewhere in the Bible: Issachar, with tens of thousands of warriors listed by genealogy; a partial record of Benjamin's sons (a fuller account of Benjamin follows in chapter 8); a single verse for all of Naphtali; the descendants of Manasseh who settled in Gilead; Ephraim, whose record pauses for real, personal tragedy, the deaths of two sons killed while raiding livestock, his father's extended mourning, and the birth of another son named Beriah, a name recalling that very tragedy; and finally Asher, closing with a substantial list of warriors and chiefs. A reader naturally asks why some tribes get so little space compared to Judah or Levi, and why the story stops, however briefly, to dwell on Ephraim's grief in the middle of a long list of names.
basic.hermeneutics: This is genealogical record punctuated once, briefly but movingly, by real narrative: Ephraim's loss and the birth of Beriah (7:20-23). The uneven space given to each tribe, one verse for Naphtali, ten for Manasseh, ten for Ephraim, is not a statement about which tribes mattered to God, since even the shortest entries record real names before Him; rather, it likely reflects how much genealogical material had survived and was available to the Chronicler by the time of writing, centuries after the tribal allotments themselves.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is threaded with mishpachah, clan, groupings and repeated tallies of gibbor chayil, mighty men of valor, a phrase that recurs for Issachar, Benjamin, and Asher alike, insisting that these lesser-known tribes were nonetheless strong and real. At its emotional center, Ephraim's avel, mourning, for his slain sons is recorded with unusual tenderness, and the shem, name, he gives his next son, Beriah, meaning tragedy, turns grief itself into a spoken, lasting memorial rather than something hidden or forgotten.
basic.typology: Ephraim's grief, followed by the birth of a son whose very name recalls the tragedy that preceded him, anticipates the pattern seen throughout Scripture of sorrow giving way, in God's timing, to new life and new hope, a pattern that finds its fullest expression in the sorrow of the cross giving way to the joy of the resurrection.
basic.christ: Though this chapter's names rarely reappear later in Scripture, Joshua, listed here in Ephraim's line (7:27) as the son of Nun, is the very Joshua who led Israel into the promised land, himself a namesake and forerunner of Jesus, since Joshua and Jesus share the same underlying Hebrew name, Yehoshua, meaning the LORD saves. Ephraim's mourning followed by a son whose name carries the memory of tragedy also points toward the pattern the gospel completes: real, named grief that does not go unacknowledged, met at last by God's own greater answer to sorrow in the resurrection of Christ.
basic.god: God is shown here as a God who records real grief alongside real genealogy: Ephraim's mourning for his sons is not glossed over or skipped past but is written into Israel's sacred record with the same care given to any king's lineage. This is a God who does not merely count names abstractly but attends to the sorrow and loss woven through ordinary family histories, and who allows even a name born from tragedy, Beriah, to carry forward into the ongoing story of His people.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 46:20-25 provides the earlier record of the sons of Ephraim, Manasseh, Naphtali, and Asher that this chapter expands. Numbers 26 gives a wilderness-era census of these same tribes. Matthew 1:1 and Acts 7:45 identify Joshua, named in this chapter's line of Ephraim, as bearing the same name later given to Jesus, both meaning the LORD saves.
basic.oneStory: Even the tribes that receive the least space in Scripture, Issachar, Naphtali, and the rest, are recorded here with real names, real warriors, and, in Ephraim's case, real and personal grief. For a community returned from exile, uncertain which tribes and families still had any place in Israel's story, this chapter's insistence on remembering every tribe, however briefly, however painfully, is a small but real assurance that no part of God's people, and no sorrow they carried, falls outside His notice, a notice fulfilled at last in Christ, who bears every sorrow and completes every broken family's hope.

context.literary: This chapter has an uneven, almost hurried shape compared to the extended treatment given to Judah and Levi: a single verse suffices for Naphtali (7:13), while Ephraim and Manasseh each receive about ten verses, and the whole chapter closes with Asher's more substantial list. This unevenness itself communicates something about the Chronicler's sources and priorities, most of the book's real theological weight rests on Judah's royal line and Levi's priestly line, while the other tribes are honored with a place in the record without receiving the same extended treatment.
context.historical: By the time Chronicles was written, ten of Israel's twelve tribes, including most of those named in this chapter, had been scattered by the Assyrian conquest of the northern kingdom more than a century before Judah's own Babylonian exile, so many of these tribal lines could no longer be traced through living communities in the land. Recording them here, even briefly, preserves their place in Israel's story and quietly insists that 'all Israel,' a phrase Chronicles uses often, still includes tribes whose descendants the returned community might never meet again.
context.canonical: Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, and Asher were all sons of Jacob through Bilhah, Zilpah, or, in Ephraim and Manasseh's case, through Joseph, so their inclusion here, however brief, completes the roll call of Jacob's descendants (alongside Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah already covered, and Benjamin, Dan, and Zebulun to follow or only partially covered). Joshua's appearance in the line of Ephraim (7:27) ties this genealogy directly to the conquest narrative, since Joshua himself was an Ephraimite, Numbers 13:8, chosen to lead Israel into the very land these genealogies describe them as inhabiting.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To preserve, however unevenly, a genealogical record of Issachar, Benjamin, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, and Asher, tribes largely scattered by the earlier Assyrian exile, so that the restored community would know that God's record of 'all Israel' extended beyond Judah and Levi to include tribes whose living presence in the land could no longer be assumed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter records specific family histories, military musters, and one particular family's grief; it does not prescribe genealogical record-keeping as a spiritual discipline for believers today, nor does it suggest that tribes given less textual space mattered less to God. What it teaches enduringly is that God's people, even those long scattered or reduced to a bare list of names, remain part of His story, and that real human grief, like Ephraim's, belongs honestly within the record of faith rather than being edited out of it.

debates.question: Why does Ephraim's personal tragedy (7:20-23) receive extended narrative treatment while most other tribes in this chapter receive only bare lists of names?

views.label: The Chronicler preserved an available, striking family tradition
views.case: It is likely that this particular story survived in Israel's collective memory or written sources with unusual vividness, perhaps because Ephraim was Joseph's son and a leading northern tribe, and the Chronicler, working from whatever records he had, simply included what had been preserved rather than omitting a moving, well-known episode.

views.label: The story functions deliberately to humanize the whole genealogical section
views.case: Placed roughly at the midpoint of the long genealogies spanning chapters 1 through 9, this rare narrative pause reminds readers that every name in every list represents a real family with real losses, joys, and griefs, not merely data; it functions as an interpretive key inviting readers to feel the human weight behind even the driest lists surrounding it.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the passage is a deliberate, meaningful inclusion rather than filler, and both invite readers to recognize the real human lives, complete with real sorrow, behind every name recorded in these opening chapters.

debates.question: What should Christians take from long, uneven genealogies like this one, where some tribes receive many verses and others almost none?

views.label: A lesson in the danger of comparing our significance to others
views.case: If even a whole tribe of Israel could be reduced to a single verse (7:13) without thereby mattering less to God, believers should resist measuring their own worth or calling by how much attention or recognition they receive compared to others; faithfulness, not visibility, is what God records and values.

views.label: A lesson in trusting God's larger, often hidden, purposes
views.case: The unevenness reflects incomplete historical records rather than divine favoritism; readers are reminded that Scripture itself does not claim to give a complete account of every person's story, and that God's care for those whose stories are barely told, like Naphtali here, is no less real for being undocumented in detail.

debates.bearing: Both lessons converge on the same pastoral point: a life recorded in only a line, or in no line at all that others can see, is not therefore unseen or unvalued by God, who kept perfect account of every tribe named in this chapter, however briefly.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's rhythm is set by repeated mishpachah, clan, headings and by the recurring tally of gibbor chayil, mighty men of valor, for Issachar, Benjamin, and Asher, a phrase insisting these tribes were strong and real even though history remembers them less than Judah or Levi. Its emotional heart is Ephraim's avel, mourning, for his slain sons Ezer and Elead, comforted by relatives who came to him, before he names his next son with a shem, a name, that itself means tragedy, Beriah, so that the family's grief became a permanent, spoken part of Israel's own family record rather than something quietly erased.

typology.type: Joshua, of the line of Ephraim, who led Israel into the promised land
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, who shares Joshua's very name, meaning the LORD saves, and leads His people into the true and lasting rest, Hebrews 4:8-9.

typology.type: Ephraim's grief giving way to the birth of a son who carried forward the family line
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of sorrow transformed into new life and hope, fulfilled most fully in the resurrection of Christ following His death, John 16:20-22.

typology.type: Scattered tribes, some reduced to a single verse, still recorded and remembered
typology.fulfillment: God's promise to gather and remember His scattered people from every tribe, fulfilled in the innumerable multitude before the throne in Revelation 7:9.

study.christ: Tucked into the line of Ephraim is a name that quietly reaches forward into the Gospels: 'Nun his son, and Joshua his son' (7:27). This is the same Joshua who succeeded Moses and led Israel into the promised land, and his Hebrew name, Yehoshua, is the very name later given to Jesus, both meaning the LORD saves. Where Joshua led Israel into a physical land of rest after the wilderness, Jesus, bearing the same name and completing what Joshua's conquest could only foreshadow, leads His people into a greater and lasting rest, Hebrews 4:8-9. And Ephraim's own story, deep grief followed by the birth of a son who carried the family forward, however marked by memory of tragedy, anticipates the gospel's own pattern: real sorrow, honestly named, that God does not erase but transforms, at the cross and the empty tomb, into everlasting joy.
study.god: God is shown here as the God who keeps account of every tribe descended from Israel, whether given ten verses or one, and who does not hide the real grief of a father like Ephraim behind a tidy list of names. He is present in both the counting of Issachar's thousands of warriors and in the comfort brought to Ephraim by his relatives, a God attentive to both the strength of nations and the sorrow of a single household, and faithful to carry His purposes forward even through a family's darkest days.

crossReferences.note: The earlier record of the sons of Joseph (Ephraim and Manasseh), Naphtali, and the sons of Bilhah and Zilpah's line, expanded here.
A wilderness-era census of Manasseh, Ephraim, Asher, and Naphtali, complementing the genealogies of this chapter.
Joshua's own tribal identity as an Ephraimite, the same line traced in verse 27.
Joshua's incomplete rest for Israel contrasted with the greater rest Jesus, bearing the same name, provides for His people.

study.oneStory: Even Israel's less prominent tribes, Issachar, Benjamin, Naphtali, Manasseh, Ephraim, and Asher, are folded honestly into the record here, warriors and all, alongside one family's raw and unhidden grief. For the returned exiles, wondering which tribes and families still counted for anything after generations of scattering and loss, this chapter answers quietly but firmly: every tribe is remembered, every name matters, and even sorrow, like Ephraim's, has its place in the ongoing story that leads, through Joshua's namesake, to Jesus Christ, in whom every scattered tribe will one day be gathered and every grief finally answered.

quiz.q: What is unusual about the space this chapter gives to Naphtali compared to Ephraim or Manasseh?

options.text: Naphtali receives only a single verse, while Manasseh and Ephraim each receive about ten, an unevenness likely reflecting available records rather than differing divine favor.
options.note: Verse 13 gives Naphtali's entire genealogy in one line, in sharp contrast to the fuller treatment of neighboring tribes.

options.text: All the tribes in this chapter receive exactly equal space.
options.note: The chapter is markedly uneven, from one verse for Naphtali to about ten each for Manasseh, Ephraim, and Asher.

options.text: Naphtali is omitted from the chapter entirely.
options.note: Naphtali is named and given a full verse listing four sons, not omitted.

options.text: Naphtali receives more space than any other tribe in the chapter.
options.note: The opposite is true; Naphtali receives the least space of any tribe named in the chapter.

quiz.q: What tragedy struck Ephraim's family, and how did he respond?

options.text: His sons Ezer and Elead were killed while stealing livestock from the natives of Gath, and Ephraim mourned for many days before his relatives came to comfort him.
options.note: Verses 21-22 record both the deaths and Ephraim's extended mourning.

options.text: His sons died of illness, and Ephraim refused to be comforted by anyone.
options.note: The cause of death was violence during a raid, and the text specifically notes his relatives came to comfort him.

options.text: His sons were killed, though the text gives no indication of his emotional response.
options.note: The text explicitly and movingly records his mourning, a rare emotional note within the genealogies.

options.text: His sons were killed in a battle Ephraim himself led against Gath.
options.note: The text specifies they were killed while trying to steal livestock, not in a battle Ephraim led.

quiz.q: Why did Ephraim name his next son Beriah?

options.text: Because tragedy had come upon his house, the name itself carrying the memory of his sons' deaths.
options.note: Verse 23 and the BSB footnote explain that Beriah sounds like the Hebrew word for tragedy or disaster.

options.text: Because Beriah means joy, celebrating the birth as leaving all sorrow behind.
options.note: The name's meaning is tied to tragedy, not joy, deliberately preserving the memory of what had happened.

options.text: Because it was simply a common name in his clan with no particular significance.
options.note: The text explicitly ties the name to the specific tragedy that preceded the birth.

options.text: Because Ephraim wished to honor his deceased sons' memory, though the text does not explain the name's meaning.
options.note: The text does explain the name's meaning directly, tying it to the household's tragedy, which this option denies.

quiz.q: Which notable biblical figure appears in the genealogy of Ephraim in this chapter?

options.text: Joshua, listed as the son of Nun, the very Joshua who later led Israel into the promised land.
options.note: Verse 27 names 'Nun his son, and Joshua his son,' identifying Joshua's place in Ephraim's line.

options.text: Moses, listed as a descendant of Ephraim.
options.note: Moses belonged to the tribe of Levi, not Ephraim; Joshua is the notable figure named here.

options.text: David, listed among Ephraim's descendants.
options.note: David belonged to the tribe of Judah, already covered in a previous section, not Ephraim.

options.text: A leader named Joshua, though the text does not identify him further.
options.note: The context, coming from Ephraim's line through Nun, clearly identifies him as the well-known successor of Moses.

quiz.q: What is the overall theological effect of this chapter's uneven, sometimes very brief, tribal records?

options.text: It shows that every tribe descended from Israel, however scattered or briefly recorded, still has a place in God's record and story.
options.note: Even the single verse given to Naphtali still preserves real names before God, part of the chapter's larger point.

options.text: It proves that only the tribes given the most verses actually mattered to God.
options.note: The chapter's inclusion of even a single verse for Naphtali argues against measuring worth by space given.

options.text: It shows the Chronicler considered these tribes unimportant and included them reluctantly.
options.note: The careful, honest inclusion of even minor details, including Ephraim's grief, argues against reluctance or dismissal.

options.text: It records the tribes accurately, though without any larger theological point being made.
options.note: Given the book's larger purpose of assuring 'all Israel' of their place in God's ongoing story, the varying space is not without theological significance.

questions: Naphtali is summed up in a single verse, yet still recorded by name before God. Where in your life do you feel like you receive less attention or recognition than others, and how does this passage reassure you that God's record of you is not measured the same way?
Ephraim's grief over his sons is recorded honestly, without being smoothed over or hidden. What grief are you carrying that you have not yet allowed others, or God, to see and comfort?

Ephraim named his next son after the very tragedy his family had suffered, refusing to erase the memory even in a moment of new life. How might naming your own pain honestly, rather than pretending it away, help you move forward in faith?

Joshua, whose name means the LORD saves, appears quietly in Ephraim's genealogy, later leading Israel into the promised land, foreshadowing Jesus who bears his name. Where in your life do you need to trust Jesus, our greater Joshua, to lead you into the rest and hope He promises?

Even scattered, mostly forgotten tribes are folded honestly into Israel's family record here. How does knowing you belong to God's larger family, even when your own story feels small or overlooked, shape how you face this week?

translationNotes.note: An em dash in the source before 'four in all' was rendered here as a comma.
An em dash in the source before 'five in all' was rendered here as a comma.
An em dash in the source before '20,200 mighty men of valor' was rendered here as a comma.
An em dash in the source before 'the descendants of Bilhah' was rendered here as a comma.
The BSB footnote explains that the name Beriah sounds like the Hebrew word for tragedy or disaster, the very meaning Ephraim intended by it.
An em dash in the source before 'heads of their families' was rendered here as a comma.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 7:1-40. Several em dashes in the source (7:1, 7:7, 7:9, 7:13, 7:40) were converted to commas; none remain in this file.
